ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a dedicated 'wholegrain cereal' term; 'cereal food product' (FOODON_03301710) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Grain milling products — wholegrain cereal (breakfast cereal, wholegrain type)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers grain milling products; used as the closest defensible anchor for a fortified wholegrain breakfast cereal. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this specific product category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No defensible SNOMED CT code identified for generic wholegrain cereal; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of wholegrain cereal is approximately 40 g (about 4–5 tablespoons of dry cereal), usually consumed with milk or a dairy-free alternative at breakfast.

nutrient_source.title: Wholegrain Cereal — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for wholegrain cereal (cr_id F05050021). All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported in the HPB record are set to -1 per numeric hygiene rules.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 66 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for wholegrain breakfast cereal (e.g., Weet-Bix / wholegrain wheat-based cereal, GI ~66–69, medium category). The specific HPB-listed product does not have a direct GI entry; the closest analogous wholegrain cereal from the University of Sydney database is used. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (66 × 30.1) / 100 ≈ 19.8 for a 40 g serving (carbohydrate = 75.27 × 0.40 = 30.1 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (26 g/100 g) with low potassium (24 mg/100 g) and moderate sodium (249 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this high-sugar wholegrain cereal because excess dietary sugar independently elevates blood pressure via insulin-driven sodium retention, and the very low potassium content (24 mg/100 g) provides no meaningful counter-regulatory offset to the 249 mg/100 g sodium load; unsweetened wholegrain cereal variants with potassium ≥200 mg/100 g are strongly preferred.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high sugar content (26 g/100 g) and high total carbohydrate (75.3 g/100 g) with medium GI (~66)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this cereal because its sugar content of 26 g/100 g far exceeds the recommended 15 g/100 g threshold for diabetic-suitable cereals, and the high carbohydrate load (75.3 g/100 g) combined with a medium GI of ~66 will produce significant postprandial glucose excursions even with 6.1 g/100 g dietary fibre present; unsweetened wholegrain cereals with sugar <5 g/100 g are recommended instead.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar (26 g/100 g) raises CVD risk; low total fat (3.97 g/100 g) and wholegrain fibre (6.06 g/100 g) are partially protective
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease, the high sugar content (26 g/100 g) is a significant concern as excess sugar raises triglycerides and promotes endothelial inflammation, partially offsetting the cardiovascular benefit of wholegrain fibre (6.1 g/100 g) and low total fat (3.97 g/100 g); choosing an unsweetened wholegrain cereal with sugar <5 g/100 g would substantially improve the cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar content (26 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this cereal because refined sugar at 26 g/100 g exceeds the 15 g/100 g RA threshold and promotes systemic inflammatory mediators; the fortified calcium (386 mg/100 g) is beneficial for patients on corticosteroids but does not offset the sugar-driven inflammation, and unsweetened wholegrain cereal variants are strongly preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wholegrain cereal is a ready-to-eat, processed breakfast cereal made primarily from wholegrains such as wholegrain wheat or oats, typically fortified with vitamins and minerals including calcium (386 mg/100 g) and iron (15 mg/100 g). It is consumed dry or with milk or a plant-based alternative, most commonly at breakfast. The texture is crisp and crunchy when dry, softening when soaked in liquid. Nutritionally, it provides 387 kcal/100 g, 11.22 g protein, 3.97 g fat, and 75.27 g carbohydrate per 100 g, with 6.06 g dietary fibre. However, the sugar content is notably high at 26.01 g/100 g, which raises significant clinical concerns. Despite the wholegrain base and moderate fibre content, this product is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis due to the high sugar load. The glycemic index is approximately 66 (medium), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 19.8 per standard 40 g serving. Sodium is 249 mg/100 g and potassium is very low at 24 mg/100 g, making the sodium-to-potassium ratio unfavourable for blood pressure management. Clinicians and dietitians should advise patients to select unsweetened wholegrain cereal variants with sugar below 5 g/100 g for improved metabolic and cardiovascular outcomes.
common_user_queries: Is wholegrain cereal good for high blood pressure?
How many calories in wholegrain cereal?
Is wholegrain cereal high in sugar?
What is the glycemic index of wholegrain cereal?
Is wholegrain cereal suitable for weight loss?
How much fibre is in wholegrain cereal?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05050021, confidence 0.95 in source record). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for the closest wholegrain cereal equivalent, reducing overall confidence to high for macros but medium for GI. Overall record confidence is rated high given the primary approved source match.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB source record): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this generic wholegrain cereal record as of 2026-04-16. Cholesterol is reported as 0.91 mg/100 g in the HPB source, which is an unusually low but non-zero value for a plant-based cereal; this is preserved as reported. No zero-macro justification is required as all core macros (protein_g 11.22, fat_g 3.97, carbohydrate_g 75.27) are non-zero. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for wholegrain breakfast cereal; the specific HPB product does not have a direct GI entry. This food is classified as generic (not a specific branded product); nutrient values may vary across commercial wholegrain cereal products. Local-language names are not applicable as this is a generic international food category with no specific cultural or regional origin requiring mandatory multilingual naming under Project Veritas v2 rules.
